content:
From subtle performance issues to hard-to-debug rendering bugs, these production failures changed how I write Angular directives today.

4 min read
2 hours ago

--

From subtle performance issues to hard-to-debug rendering bugs, these production failures changed how I write Angular directives today.
I shipped a directive that looked perfect in code review. Clean logic, small footprint, one clear responsibility. Three weeks later it was quietly re-rendering a lending dashboard forty times a second, and nobody noticed until a customer complained the page felt laggy on her phone.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Nothing had thrown an error. Nothing had failed a test. The directive just sat there, doing exactly what I wrote, which turned out to be the actual problem.
That gap between “it works” and “it is correct” is where most directive bugs live. Here are five mistakes that never showed up locally and only revealed themselves once real users, real data, and real load hit the code.
The Listener That Would Not Die
A directive that adds an event listener in ngOnInit and never removes it will outlive the element it was attached to, at least in terms of memory. Angular does…
